sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 256 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 3.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE changed 13 cells again. Let me check the segmentation to see what changed. Looking at the image, it looks similar to before - the player (green) is on the center target, and there's a blue square below the player. The charcoal frame to the right is still there.
Let me check the segmentation to understand the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N', 'c', 'G', 'b']:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The state is almost the same as before:
- Node 3: color=N, boundary=[[28, 28], [28, 31], [30, 31], [30, 28]] - green body at rows 28-30, cols 28-31
- Node 4: color=b, boundary=[[28, 32], [28, 39], [31, 39], [31, 32], ...] - blue center target at rows 28-31, cols 32-39 (18 pixels, same as before)
- Node 7: color=W, boundary=[[31, 28], [31, 31]] - white strip at row 31, cols 28-31 (4 pixels)
- Node 8: color=G, boundary=[[32, 28], [32, 31], [35, 31], [35, 28]] - dark gray frame at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 (NEW target spawned!)
- Node 9: color=b, boundary=[[33, 29], [33, 30], [34, 30], [34, 29]] - blue center at rows 33-34, cols 29-30 (inside node 8)
- Node 1: color=c, boundary=[[24, 40], [24, 43], [31, 43], [31, 40]] - charcoal frame at rows 24-31, cols 40-43
- Node 11: color=c, boundary=[[63, 46], [63, 63]] - bottom bar with 18 cells (was 17)
So pressing SPACE again spawned another target at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 (same position as before!). The center target is still 18 pixels (didn't shrink further). The bottom bar grew from 17 to 18 cells.
Wait, but the previous target at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 was collected (changed to white frame). Now a new target appeared at the same position. This suggests targets respawn at the same position after being collected, or SPACE spawns targets at fixed positions.
Let me think about this differently. The game seems to have:
1. A center target (blue, no frame) that spawns new targets when SPACE is pressed.
2. Targets (charcoal/dark gray frame + blue center) that can be collected by pressing SPACE when adjacent.
3. A bottom bar that grows as actions are taken.
The goal might be to collect all targets simultaneously (have all frames white at the same time). But targets respawn when SPACE is pressed on the center target.
Alternatively, maybe the goal is to fill the bottom bar to a certain length. The bar is at 18 cells now, and it grows by 1 cell per action. If the goal is to fill 64 cells, I'd need 46 more actions, which would take a long time.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of pressing SPACE on the center target repeatedly, let me try to collect all the existing targets first, then see what happens.
Current targets:
1. Node 1: charcoal frame at rows 24-31, cols 40-43 (24 pixels, 8 rows tall - this is two stacked targets)
2. Node 8: dark gray frame at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 (12 pixels, 4 rows tall - spawned target)
The player is at rows 28-30, cols 28-31. To collect the spawned target (node 8):
- The target is at rows 32-35, cols 28-31. The player is at rows 28-30, cols 28-31. They're adjacent (row 30 is adjacent to row 32... no, row 31 is between them).
- Actually, the player's white strip is at row 31, cols 28-31 (node 7), which is adjacent to the target at row 32. So pressing SPACE should collect the target.
Let me try pressing SPACE to collect the spawned target.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 28-30, cols 28-31 (on center target)
# Spawned target at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 (dark gray frame)
# Player's white strip at row 31, cols 28-31 is adjacent to target
# Press SPACE to collect the spawned target
action(['SPACE'], intent='solve')
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
